pages.text: Contribution of cocktail of polypropylene and steel ﬁbres in improving the behaviour of high strength concrete subjected to high temperature P. Pliya ⇑, A-L. Beaucour, A. Noumowé. Université de Cergy-Pontoise, L2MGC, EA 4114, 95000 Cergy-Pontoise, France article info Article history: Received 21 June 2010 Received in revised form 19 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 14 December 2010 Keywords: Concrete Temperature Polypropylene ﬁbres Steel ﬁbres Fibres cocktail Porosity Mass loss Strength Modulus of elasticity Ductility abstract The aim of the study is to improve the understanding of the inﬂuence of a polypropylene and steel ﬁbres cocktail on the behaviour of high strength concretes subjected to high temperature. Concrete mixes were studied by adding polypropylene ﬁbres, steel ﬁbres and cocktail of ﬁbres. The concrete specimens were subjected to various heating–cooling cycles. The initial and residual mechanical properties, the porosity and the mass loss of the studied concrete mixes were investigated. Different concretes compo- sitions with various amounts of polypropylene and/or steel ﬁbres were tested. Experimental results show the signiﬁcant improvement of the residual mechanical properties of concretes containing the cocktail of ﬁbres compared to concretes without ﬁbres. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The mechanical properties of concrete can be improved with the use of additives and ﬁne particles. High strength concrete and then ultra-high strength concrete will gradually replace nor- mal strength concrete. The main characteristic of high strength concrete and ultra-high strength concrete is their dense internal microstructure which ensures a very low permeability. Recent ﬁres in tunnels, in buildings and laboratory tests showed that heated concrete can present instability [1–10]. The instability can take place by spalling and depends on the ﬁre conditions (heating rate, temperature) and on the concrete characteristics (moisture con- tent, type of aggregate, density, porosity/permeability, specimen dimensions). According to ArupFire [10], they are many types of spalling: explosive spalling, surface spalling, aggregate splitting, corner separation, sloughing and post cooling spalling. The con- crete instability could be the result of two mechanisms: the vapour pressure build-up mechanism [11,12] and the restrained thermal dilatation mechanism [13,14]. High strength concrete is believed to be more susceptible to the pressure build up because of its low permeability, compared to that of normal strength concrete [3–6]. The dense microstructure of high strength concrete reduces the migration of liquid and vapour water. Several studies carried out by different authors [15–21] show that concrete thermal stability is improved by incorporating poly- propylene ﬁbres to the mix. Since the ﬁbres melt at approximately 160–170 /C176C, they produce expansion channels. The additional porosity and small channels created by polypropylene ﬁbres melt- ing may lower internal vapour pressure in the concrete and reduce the likelihood of spalling, according to Noumowé [20]. The addi- tional porosity due to the melting of polypropylene ﬁbres can lead to a decrease of the residual mechanical performances of concretes. Results of literature on this subject are contradictory. Several stud- ies carried out by different authors as [20,24,29] show a decrease of residual strength in agreement with the additional

2.3. Tests Heating–cooling cycles : four heating–cooling cycles were carried out in an electric furnace from the room temperature up to 150 /C176C, 300 /C176C, 450 /C176C or 600 /C176C. The cycles included a phase of rise in tem- perature, a phase of temperature dwell and a phase of cooling. The heating rate was 1 /C176Cm n/C01 and the dwell duration was one hour. The specimens were then free to cool down to room temperature in the furnace. Mass loss: the concrete mass loss was obtained on four cylindri- cal specimens Ø 160 /C2320 mm (diameter /C2height). The speci- mens were weighed before and after each heating–cooling cycle in order to determine the quantity of water that escaped from the concrete during the heating. Compressive strength (fc): the compression test was carried out on four cylindrical specimens Ø 160 /C2320 mm in accordance with NF EN 12390-3 [32] standard. The loading rate was 0.5 MPa s /C01 un- til the failure. Flexural tensile strength (ft): the tests were carried out on three prismatic specimens 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm. In accordance with NF EN 12390-5 [33] standard, the loading in two points was carried out at a speed of 0.25 mm mn /C01 until the rupture. Modulus of elasticity (E): the test was carried out on three cylin- drical specimens Ø 160 /C2320 mm. During the compression test, the specimen was connected to an extensometer frame and sub- jected to three loading–unloading cycles until reaching 30% of the breaking strength [34]. The loading rate was 0.5 MPa s /C01. Water porosity (P): we measured the total porosity and not the pore size distribution. The concrete porosity was obtained on 10 samples of average mass of 80 g. Samples resulted from the speci- mens after the mechanical tests. The samples were oven dried at a temperature of 60 /C176C. When the samples were dried, they were im- mersed in water until complete saturation. Tests were carried out on immersed samples after 4, 8 and 16 days. The results show a reduction of the saturated mass after 8 days of immersion. We ob- served a rehydration phenomenon. After keeping the samples dur- ing 8 days in water, a weighing in immersed saturated state was carried out on a hydrostatic balance then it was followed by a weighing in saturated state after the specimen was wiped with linen to remove the water in excess at the surface. The test method is slightly different from the AFREM procedures [35]. The tested samples were those brought up to temperature of 60 /C176C, 150 /C176C, 300 /C176C or 450 /C176C. Taking into account the friability of the specimens heated at the temperature of 600 /C176C, porosity was not measured on samples heated beyond 450 /C176C. 3. Concrete mixes Four groups of high strength concrete mixes were studied: one group of concrete mixes without ﬁbres (C3), one group of concrete mixes with polypropylene ﬁbres (CP3), one group of concrete mixes with steel ﬁbres (CS3) and one group of concrete mixes with polypropylene and steel ﬁbres (CSP3). All mixes have the same water/cement (W/C) ratio of 0.3 and the same paste volume. The type of concrete obtained was S4 with the slump test values be- tween 16 and 20 cm ± 1. The percentage of superplasticizer was adjusted in order to keep the workability constant. Two volume fractions of polypropylene ﬁbres in the concrete were tested: 0.11% and 0.22% (equivalent to 1 and 2 kg/m 3). Two volume frac- tions of steel ﬁbres were used: 0.38% and 0.50% (equivalent to 30 and 40 kg/m3). One part of the specimens wa

of concretes with and without PP-F or S-F. For ﬁbres cocktail con- cretes, eight cylindrical specimens Ø 160 /C2320 mm and six pris- matic specimens 100 /C2100 /C2400 mm were heated for each group. For all groups of concretes, no explosive spalling was ob- served. The only spalling phenomena observed after the various heating–cooling cycles are the aggregates splitting and corner sep- aration on prismatic specimen. Other authors like Peng et al. [28] have shown some explosive spalling of concretes with PP-F, S-F and ﬁbres cocktail. Specimens were cured in water at 20 /C176C until 28 days. The heating rate was 10 /C176Cm n/C01. The results showed a signiﬁcant explosive spalling with S-F concretes (7 on 16 beams: 6 below 200 /C176C and 1 between 200 /C176C and 400 /C176C). The initial moisture condition of specimens and the higher heating rate can explain the thermal instability of con- cretes studied by Peng et al. [28]. The proportion of the S-F in that concrete was 100 kg/m 3. Moreover, ﬁbres cocktail concretes with 70 kg/m3 of S-F and 0.3 kg/m 3 of PP-F spalled during the heating below 200 /C176C (3 on 16). But, no concrete with 40 kg/m 3 of S-F and 0.6 kg/m 3 of PP-F spalled. The amount of PP-F or S-F in con- crete can have an important effect on the concrete thermal stability. 4.2. Porosity Fig. 2 shows the evolution of relative porosity of C3, CP3 and CS3 concretes. The porosity increases with the temperature. This growth is linked to the departure of water and the cracking gener- ated by the differential thermal expansion between the cement paste, the aggregates and the ﬁbres. The porosity increases more quickly for CP3 concretes than for CS3 concretes. After the heat- ing–cooling cycle at 300 /C176C, the relative porosity was 152.7% and 119.7% for CP3-2 and CS3-30 concretes, respectively. Steel ﬁbres reinforced concretes showed a lower porosity increase compared to the polypropylene ﬁbres reinforced concretes. The fast increase of porosity can be explained by the polypropylene ﬁbres melting that created supplementary porosity in the concrete. The studies carried out by Hager and co-workers [8,16,20,24] have also shown an additional porosity related to the ﬁbres melting. The Table 2 Porosity (%) as a function of the heating temperature ( T). However, the relative residual compressive strengths of concretes with and without PP-F are similar for a heating at 600 /C176C. These re- sults are in coherence with the porosity results obtained in this study and in that carried out by other authors [8,20,24]. Indeed, the increase of the porosity and the permeability [19,29,36] observed following the addition of PP-F in concrete can explain the low residual strength of CP3 concretes. Some authors like Behnood and Ghandehari [21] and Chen and Liu [26] have obtained an increase or a slightly increase of residual compressive strength of the concretes with PP-F. It is not consis- tent with our observations. The difference between the studies can be related to the experimental conditions. Specimens were cured in water [21,26] and the heating rate was 10 /C176Cm n /C01 [26]. In the present study, specimens were not cured in water and the heating rate was 1 /C176Cm n/C01. The enhancement of permeability due to the presence of PP-F ﬁbres has an even more favourable ef- fect for saturated concrete heated at a higher rate. With the addition of steel ﬁbres, an improvement of compres- sive strength is noticed from the room temperature until the heat- ing–cooling cycle of 600 /C176C. The relative residual strength of CS3 concretes is higher than that of C3 and CP3 concretes. After the heating at 450 /C176C, more than 50% of initial strength is preserved with CS3 concretes (54.5%). For C3 and CP3 concretes, residual strengths are 38.3% and 28.5%, respectively. At 600 /C176C, the contri- bution of S-F decreases. The relative compressive strength for the heating at 600 /C176C of CS3, C3 and CP3 concretes are 19.5%, 13.2% and 11.4%, respectively. Therefore, adding steel ﬁbres to high strength concrete limits damages after exposure to high tempera- ture. The use of steel ﬁbres slightly reduced the rate of degradation of compressive strength. At 450 /C176C, the increase of the amount of S- F from 30 to 40 kg/m 3 makes it possible to improve the residual compressive strength. This favourable inﬂuence does not appear anymore at 600 /C176C. For a volume of 1% of steel ﬁbres (twice higher) at 600 /C176C, Poon et al. [27] show an improvement of the residual compressive strength of 7%. This result conﬁrms the absence of important proﬁt induced by the increasing of S-F volume at the temperature 600 /C176C. The gain of residual compressive strength ob- tained in this study is 6.3% for a concrete containing 0.5% of S-F. This improvement percentage of residual compressive strength is close to those obtained by Lau and Anson [23] and Poon et al. [27]. The impact of ﬁbres cocktail on heated concretes properties is shown in Fig. 7 . Behaviour of CSP3 concretes is close to that of CS3 concretes. A loss of 3% of relative residual compressive strength of CSP3 is noticed compared to that of CS3 concretes at the temperature of 300 /C176C. This compressive strength loss is related to the melting of the polypropylene ﬁbres which generates chan- nels in the concrete. The porosity results show an additional poros- ity in ﬁbres cocktail concretes compared to steel ﬁbres concretes. No signiﬁcant inﬂuence of different fraction volumes of the two types of ﬁbres on the residual compressive strength was noticed. When comparing the rate of strength loss for the four groups of concretes ( Table 5 ), it is observed that between 20 /C176C and 300 /C176C, the rate decreases with the addition of S-F. CSP3 concretes have a higher rate of

ene ﬁbres. Fig. 9 shows the inﬂuence of ﬁbres cocktail on the resid- ual ﬂexural tensile strength. The ﬂexural tensile strength decreases gradually with the rise in temperature for all the four concretes groups. The addition of S-F or PP-F did not inﬂuence the shape of the curve. As for the residual compressive strength, the addition of poly- propylene ﬁbres involves a reduction of the residual ﬂexural ten- sile strength, in particular at 450 /C176C. With S-F, an important gain in ﬂexural tensile strength is observed regardless the temperature. At 600 /C176C, C3, CP3 and CS3 concretes have relative ﬂexural tensile strength of 9.4%, 10.1%, and 37.3%, respectively. As for the residual compressive strength, the increase in steel ﬁbres leads to a signif- icant improvement in the ﬂexural tensile strength around 450 /C176C. At 600 /C176C, the inﬂuence of the steel ﬁbres disappears. The improve- ment of the residual ﬂexural tensile strength of concretes contain- ing 0.5% of S-F (CS3-40) is 28% at 600 /C176C. The works of Chen and Liu [26] showed a little higher improvement of the tensile strength, 40% at 600 /C176C, for a concrete containing more ﬁbres (0.60%). The improvement of the residual ﬂexural tensile strength is much bet- ter than that of compressive strength for concretes with steel ﬁbres. The results of CSP3 concretes ( Fig. 9 ) show an increase of ﬂex- ural tensile strength compared to C3 concretes but a little decrease compared to CS3. The relative ﬂexural tensile strength of CSP3 con- cretes is lower than that of CS3 concretes. This result has also been reported by Chen and co-workers [26,28–30]. At 600 /C176C, the rela- tive strength of the CSP3 concretes was 27.0%. It is 10% less than that of CS3 concretes. The loss of residual ﬂexural tensile strength can be explained by the presence of PP-F. The inﬂuence of the amount of steel ﬁbres in the concrete mixes incorporating a cock- tail of ﬁbres is more important on residual ﬂexural tensile strength than on residual compressive strength. The increase of residual ﬂexural tensile strength is 5–9% at 600 /C176C when the volume frac- tion of steel ﬁbres varies from 0.38% (30 kg/m 3) to 0.5% (40 kg/m 3). Between 20 /C176C and 300 /C176C, the steel ﬁbres limit the rate of ﬂex- ural tensile strength loss ( Table 6). The rate of relative ﬂexural ten- sile strength loss is lower for CS3 concretes than that of C3, CP3 and CSP3 concretes. Beyond the temperature of 300 /C176C, the strength loss rate is almost the same for all the concretes. Fibres cocktail concrete shows beneﬁts compared to concrete without ﬁbres and with concrete PP-F. For the amount of PP-F tested in this study (0.25% and 0.51%), the residual ﬂexural tensile strength of CSP3 concretes was especially inﬂuenced by the amount of S-F in the ﬁ- bres cocktail. 4.6. Residual ductility Steel ﬁbres are used to improve the concrete ductility at room temperature. Compression and ﬂexural tests show a quite ductile behaviour of the concrete with steel ﬁbres or ﬁbres cocktail as well to the room temperature as after the heating–cooling cycles. Fig. 10 illustrates the ductile behaviour of CSP3 concretes Fig. 8. Relative residual ﬂexural tensile strength of C3, CP3 and CS3 concretes as a function of temperature. Fig. 9. Relative residual ﬂexural tensile strength of concretes at the temperature of 300 /C176C and 600 /C176C. Table 6 Rate of relative residual ﬂexural tensile strength loss of concretes. Field Rate of strength loss (% C /C01) C3

erties were studied. Some conclusions can be drawn from the experimental results: /C15The tests results show an important increase of concrete poros- ity from the temperature of 300 /C176C. Less increase of porosity is noted for S-F concretes than for PP-F concretes. After heating at 300 /C176C, the porosity of the CSP3 concretes with 1 kg/m 3 of PP-F increases more quickly than the porosity of the CSP3 con- cretes with 2 kg/m 3. The evolution of the porosity of CSP3 con- cretes does not follow that observed with CP3 and CS3 concretes. /C15The mass of the different groups of concretes decreases with the temperature. An additional mass decrease is noticed with the concretes incorporating PP-F. After the heating at 300 /C176C and 600 /C176C, the mass loss of ﬁbres cocktail concretes is lower than that of concretes with and without PP-F but is higher than that of S-F concretes. /C15Compared with concretes without ﬁbres (C3), a reduction of rel- ative residual strength was observed with CP3 concretes while the CS3 concretes presented an increase of residual strength. After the heating–cooling cycle of 600 /C176C, the increase of rela- tive residual ﬂexural tensile strength of CS3 concrete was 27% compared with that of C3 concretes. The improvement of the residual compressive strength was 6%. S-F improved also the residual modulus of elasticity. The enhancement of relative residual modulus of elasticity of CS3 concretes was 5% at 450 /C176C. At 600 /C176C, the relative modulus of elasticity was almost the same for all the concretes groups. /C15Residual mechanical characteristics of CSP3 concretes were lower than that of CS3 concretes but higher than that of C3 con- cretes. After the heating at 300 /C176C and 600 /C176C, the relative ﬂex- ural tensile strength decrease of CSP3 concretes was about 10% compared with that of CS3 concretes. Residual compressive strength and modulus of elasticity of CSP3 varied slightly with steel ﬁbres content. A keeping of the concrete residual ductility by incorporating ﬁbres cocktail was also noticed. This study shows the improvement of the residual mechanical properties of concretes incorporating ﬁbres cocktail. Two heat- ing–cooling cycles were tested at a heating rate of 1 /C176Cm n/C01. For the residual porosity of ﬁbres cocktail concretes only one heat- ing–cooling cycle and one heating rate were operated. It would be necessary to test other heating–cooling cycles and heating rates in order to have completed understanding of the inﬂuence of the two ﬁbres cocktail. References [1] Hertz K. [7] Phan LT, Lawson JR, Davis FL. Effects of elevated temperature exposure on hea

microstructure modiﬁcation of CP3 concretes was more noticeable than that of CS3 concretes. The relative porosity after the heating at 300 /C176C of ﬁbres cocktail reinforced concretes was compared to that of C3, CP3 and CS3 con- cretes (Fig. 3). The ﬁbres cocktail concretes porosity increases with the temperature. The relative porosity of CSP3 concretes, with 1 kg/m3 of PP-F, is higher than that of C3, CP3 and CS3 concretes. The relative porosity was 127.9%, 119.7% and 138.6% for CP3-1, CS3-30 and CSP3-30 /C21, respectively. The contrary phenomenon was observed with 2 kg/m 3 of PP-F in the mixture. The relative porosity of CSP3 concretes was lower than that of CP3 and CS3 con- cretes. The relative porosity was 152.7%, 107.3% and 110.8% for CP3-2, CS3-40 and CSP3-40 /C22, respectively. This result is ob- served for the two amounts of S-F, 30 and 40 kg/m 3. 4.3. Concrete mass loss Fig. 4 shows the effect of the addition of PP-F or S-F in the con- cretes on the mass loss as a function of the heating temperature. The standard deviation of the mass loss varied from 0.0% to 0.4%. The effect of ﬁbres cocktail in the concretes is shown on Fig. 5 . The addition of PP-F, S-F or ﬁbres cocktail does not affect the con- crete mass loss evolution. For CP3 concretes, a little increase of the mass loss compared to that of C3 concretes is observed regardless the temperature. An additional mass loss is thus observed with the addition of PP-F in concrete. At 450 /C176C, the average mass losses were 6.3% for CP3 concretes and 6.1% for C3 concretes. This differ- ence appears higher than the average mass proportion of the PP-F in the concretes (0.06%). On Fig. 4 , it can be noted that mass loss of CS3 concretes are lower to that of C3 and CP3 concretes. The steel ﬁbres concretes were obtained by replacement of part of the aggregates by an equivalent volume of ﬁbres. So the difference in mass loss could correspond to water contained in these aggregates. The mass loss difference between C3 and CS3 concretes is almost constant until 450 /C176C; the mass loss of CS3 concretes is only 5.5% instead of 6.1% for C3 concretes. After the heating–cooling cycle of 600 /C176C, the mass loss of the two groups of concretes are similar, with a mass loss of 7.2% for C3 concretes and an average mass loss of 7.0% for CS3 concretes. Fig. 5 shows the evolution of the mass loss of CSP3 concretes compared to that of C3 and CS3 concretes. The results of the mass loss of CSP3 concretes were not compared to that of CP3 concretes because the two compositions of concretes do not contain the same volume of aggregates. CSP3 concretes ( Fig. 5 ) show higher mass loss compared to that of CS3 concretes. The difference is re- lated to polypropylene ﬁbres. The mass loss varies slightly with the amount of polypropylene ﬁbres. After the heating–cooling cy- cle of 600 /C176C, the mass losses of CSP3-40 /C21 and CSP3-40 /C22 con- cretes were 7.2% and 7.4%, respectively. 4.4. Residual compressive strength Fig. 6 shows the evolution of relative residual compressive strength of C3, CP3 and CS3 concretes. Fig. 7 illustrates the effect of the ﬁbres cocktail. For the four groups of concretes, residual compressive strength decreases with the temperature. PP-F and S-F do not inﬂuence the general shape of the curves ( Fig. 6 ). Relative residual compressive strength of CP3 concretes is lower than that of C3 concretes ( Fig. 6 ). The addition of 2 kg/m 3 of PP-F involves an average strength loss of 24% fo

inﬂuence on the variation of modulus of elasticity with the temper- ature, according to Cheng et al. [22]. A good mechanical contribu- tion of steel ﬁbres for the temperature lower than 1000 /C176C was noted by Lau and Anson [23]. The thermal stability can be inﬂu- enced by the presence of steel ﬁbres. According to Rivaz [25], the presence of micropores around the steel ﬁbres allows the reduction of vapour pressure during the heating. Some authors like Chen and Liu [26] and Peng et al. [28] have obtained thermal instabilities with concretes containing steel ﬁbres. According to Chen and Liu [26], steel ﬁbres can delay the time when spalling occurs and polypropylene ﬁbres can eliminate the spalling under high temperature. Polypropylene and steel ﬁbres in concrete can be used to reduce spalling and to enhance the residual strength of heated concretes. Few studies were carried out on the concretes [26–30] or mortar [31], with polypropylene and steel ﬁbres at high temperature. The results obtained by different authors appear contradictory. The works of [26–28,30] underline a notable improvement of the residual mechanical performances of the cocktail ﬁbres concretes compared to that of the concretes without ﬁbres. The results ob- tained by Suhaendi and Takashi [29] show, on the contrary, little variation or a reduction of the residual mechanical strength. The relative amount of polypropylene ﬁbres or steel ﬁbres into the cocktail of ﬁbres is an important factor. Today, the available data in the literature correspond to a small range of ﬁbres combination and tested with different experimental conditions. It is thus difﬁ- cult, by comparing the various results, to conclude on the inﬂuence of the relative amount of polypropylene ﬁbres and steel ﬁbres on the improvement of the concretes residual mechanical properties. In general, it can be concluded that there is inconsistent infor- mations on the mechanical and physical properties of high strength concretes containing polypropylene ﬁbres, steel ﬁbres or a cocktail of ﬁbres. Furthermore, little information has been re- ported on the mass loss, the porosity, the residual strengths and modulus of elasticity of high strength concretes with a cocktail of ﬁbres. This paper provides new experimental data to improve the understanding of the behaviour at high temperature of high strength concrete reinforced with a cocktail of ﬁbres. The study is carried out in two stages. Firstly, behaviour of high strength con- cretes without ﬁbres is compared to that of high strength concretes with polypropylene ﬁbres and to that of high strength concretes with steel ﬁbres. The inﬂuence of two amounts of polypropylene and steel ﬁbres is studied. Then the behaviour of high strength con- cretes with a cocktail of polypropylene and steel ﬁbres is investi- gated. Different relative volume fractions of the two types of ﬁbres are tested. This systematic approach allows to distinguish the inﬂuence of each type of ﬁbre in the ﬁbres cocktail concrete and to better understand the impact of the ﬁbres cocktail on the thermo-mechanical behaviour of concrete. Concretes mixes without ﬁbres, concretes mixes with polypro- pylene ﬁbres, concretes mixes with steel ﬁbres and concretes mixes with a cocktail of ﬁbres were prepared. Specimens were subjected to various heating–cooling cycles from the room temper- ature to 150 /C176C, 300 /C176C, 450 /C176C and 600 /C176C. The compressive strength, the ﬂexural tensile strength and the modulus
